Just now

--

A Meditation on Current, Communion, and the Holy Mystery of the Middle
Press enter or click to view image in full size

A transistor is a small thing. So small that most of us never see one directly. It is hidden inside radios, computers, phones, amplifiers, instruments, machines, pacemakers, satellites, toys, cars, microphones, memory, screens, and the strange glowing nervous system of modern life. It does not usually receive our gratitude. We do not light candles before it. We do not sing hymns to it. We do not notice that civilization hums through billions upon billions of tiny gates and junctions, each one deciding whether current may pass, whether signal may be strengthened, whether silence may become sound.
And yet, within the transistor there is a parable.
Not a proof of God. Not a machine that explains the Holy. No metaphor can do that. The divine mystery is not trapped inside silicon any more than God is trapped inside a temple, a doctrine, a painting, a poem, or a star. But creation has a way of whispering. Matter has a way of becoming icon. The smallest structures sometimes reveal the deepest patterns. A seed, a cell, a flame, a wave, a breath, a word, a wound, a body, a table, a loaf, a cup — all of these have carried theological weight. Why not a transistor?
